title: Best Wood Filler of 2026: For Paint, Stain & Rot Repairs
excerpt: Find a wood filler that won't fail under stain or crumble in the can. Our picks tackle paint, stain, and exterior rot with the actual durability you need.
author: Editorial Team
metaDescription: Find a wood filler that won't fail under stain or crumble in the can. Our picks tackle paint, stain, and exterior rot with the actual durability you need.
intro:
Many wood fillers promise stainability or all-weather toughness, but the reality is often a blotchy finish or a patch that crumbles. This guide cuts through the gap to match fillers to your actual project—paint, stain, or exterior rot—so you don't waste time on a product that looks good in the tube but fails under finish.

Whether you're filling nail holes in painted trim, repairing rotted window sills, or matching stain on fine furniture, the wrong filler will show. The right one disappears into the wood, accepting the same stain or paint as the surrounding surface without cracking or blistering.

We've identified the fillers that actually deliver on these promises, from smooth-sanding latex pastes to structural epoxies. Each pick earns its place by handling the material and finish demands of its specific use case—because no single filler works for every job.

products.title: FamoWood 40022126 Latex Wood Filler - Pint, Natural, 24 oz.
products.label: FamoWood Latex Filler 24 oz
products.oneLiner: Versatile 24-oz tub dries in 15 minutes for flawless paint-grade repairs.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: For deep voids, apply in thin layers, letting each dry fully to prevent cracking.
pros: Effortless spreading and feathering, sands to a fine powder with minimal work. — Ease of use 116 mentions, 89% positive; Sanding 97 mentions, 95% positive
Dries quickly with negligible shrinkage, leaving a hard, paintable surface. — Drying time 140 mentions, 84% positive; Quality 132 mentions, 98% positive
Solvent-free, low-odor formula cleans up with water—no fumes or harsh chemicals. — Users praise the low odor and water wash-up
Holds up on both interior and exterior painted wood, economical for large projects. — Functionality 113 mentions, 91% positive
cons: Deep fills may crack if applied in a single thick layer; building up thin coats is necessary for a durable repair. — Durability: 71 mentions, 34% negative — cracks reported on thick applications

Stain absorption is inconsistent; patches may appear lighter or darker than the wood, limiting the filler to painted surfaces. — 70 mentions, 36% negative — not as stainable as claimed, best for paint
products.verdict: For paint-grade wood repairs from nail holes to gouges, this 24 oz tub delivers quick sanding and smooth results at a mid-range price—just don’t expect it to double as a stainable filler.
products.review:
This latex wood filler spreads like smooth butter and sands to a fine powder with minimal effort. It feathers to a virtually invisible edge, so repairs blend into the surrounding surface with little sanding. The filler dries in about 15 minutes for thin fills, cures rock hard with almost no shrinkage, and cleans up with plain water—no harsh solvents. The 24 oz tub covers a lot of ground on baseboards, door frames, and furniture without breaking the bank. It also holds up on exterior painted surfaces, handling window sills and fascia boards without cracking under weather exposure.

On stain-grade projects, the filler’s absorption is uneven; repairs appear lighter or darker than the surrounding wood. Water cleanup and low odor make it pleasant to use indoors. Thin, shallow repairs are where this filler truly shines; the fast dry time keeps projects moving. Once painted, filled areas blend seamlessly, and the easy sanding makes the prep work fast.

This filler suits DIYers and painters tackling nail holes, dents, and shallow cracks on trim, paneling, or cabinetry that will get a coat of paint. It’s not the right choice for deep voids that need a single-fill solution, or for wood that will be stained rather than painted. If you need a stainable filler with truer color acceptance, a premium solvent-based alternative may be a better match.

💡 💡 Tip: For deep voids, apply in thin layers, letting each dry fully to prevent cracking.

products.title: Timbermate Maple/Beech/Pine Hardwood Wood Filler 8Oz Jar
products.label: Timbermate Maple 8 oz
products.oneLiner: Water-based putty that truly accepts stain, blending invisibly with maple, beech, or pine.
pros: Stain acceptance blends seamlessly into maple, beech, and pine with minimal witness lines — Quality 73 mentions, 93% positive; Color match 20 mentions, 95% positive
Sands exceptionally smooth and dries hard without cracking or crumbling — Sanding 22 mentions, 95% positive; Drying time 42 mentions, 81% positive
Water-based formula allows easy cleanup and re-wetting with water — Water-based feature praised by users
cons: Higher cost per ounce than basic paint-grade fillers — Higher cost compared to latex fillers
Not waterproof, so it cannot be used for outdoor or high-moisture repairs — not water resistant
products.verdict: Woodworkers who demand invisible stain-matched repairs on maple, beech, or pine will accept the higher cost and indoor limitation for the truest stain acceptance in a water-based filler.
products.review:
Timbermate accepts stain more convincingly than any water-based filler in this category, blending into maple, beech, and pine with minimal witness lines. It sands to a smooth, hard finish without shrinking or cracking, and the water-based formula cleans up easily and can be re-wetted if needed. Unlike the paint-grade all-rounder filler, this putty is built for stain-grade work where the repair must disappear after finishing.

For fine woodworking and cabinetry repairs where stain matching is critical, the strong chemical odor is a tradeoff — it demands a well-ventilated workspace and is not suitable for small, enclosed areas. It also cannot be used outdoors or in high-moisture settings. Woodworkers who accept these boundaries get a filler that delivers true stain-matching performance, but casual DIYers may find the price steep for the 8-ounce jar.

products.title: 3M Bondo Wood Filler 30 oz (850.49 g) with Bondo Cream Hardner 0.5 oz (14g), Repairs Chips and Cracks in Wood, Sandable, Paintable, and Stainable (WF-QT-ES)
products.label: Bondo Wood Filler (30 oz)
products.badge: Best for Exterior Rot & Large Repairs
products.oneLiner: 30-oz two-part filler sands in 15 minutes, forming a rot-proof exterior patch.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: Mix golf-ball-sized portions and spread immediately — the working window barely lasts five minutes.
pros: Forms a permanent, structural repair that outlasts the surrounding wood in exterior applications. — Effectiveness 270 mentions, 88% positive
After a quick 15-minute cure, the filler sands to a feather edge and accepts paint without peeling. — Sandability 85 mentions, 89% positive; Durability 78 mentions, 79% positive

Its thick, moldable consistency fills deep gaps and rotted-out sections in one pass, eliminating the need for multiple layers. — Mixability 70 mentions, 71% positive; success on window sills and decks
cons: Strong chemical odor lingers; open air or a respirator are needed for indoor use. — Lingering chemical smell noted in reviews
Sets within minutes, demanding immediate spread — no time for fine sculpting. — 183 mentions, 43% negative on curing speed
products.verdict: For exterior rot repair that demands a paint-ready, permanent fix in one afternoon, Bondo fills the gap where more forgiving fillers would fail.
products.review:
Where standard wood fillers stop at small cracks and nail holes, this two-part resin filler tackles rotted window sills, door frames, and deck boards. It hardens to a sandable, paint-ready surface in 15 minutes — a pace that sets it apart from slower epoxy pastes used for similar repairs. The permanent bond fills large gaps without shrinking, holding up where water repeatedly attacks.

Paint-grade exterior restoration is its calling; for interior woodwork or stain-grade projects, the rapid set time and strong fumes become serious constraints. The thin, spreadable consistency demands confident, quick application — mixing more than a golf-ball-sized batch can cure before it reaches the wood. If you repair a rotted porch post once rather than replace it, this filler's value is clear.

💡 💡 Tip: Mix golf-ball-sized portions and spread immediately — the working window barely lasts five minutes.

products.title: J-B Weld Tan KwikWood Wood Repair Epoxy Putty, 1 oz. Stick
products.label: J-B Weld KwikWood Stick
products.badge: Best Epoxy Stick for Instant Fixes
products.oneLiner: Hand-kneadable stick cures in 1 hour with 900 PSI strength for stripped screws.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: Have the repair area prepped and shape the putty immediately; you have roughly 15 minutes to work before it stiffens.
pros: Fills stripped screw holes and anchors hardware with 900 PSI strength — 506 mentions, 86% positive
Kneads and shapes easily for a precise fit in small repairs — 293 mentions, 82% positive
Sands and drills to a hard, paintable finish in under an hour — 293 mentions, 81% positive on quality; 96 mentions on sandability
cons: Kneading and curing releases a strong chemical odor that lingers — intense negative odor
The 15-25 minute working window leaves little time for shaping on larger repairs — hardens too quickly to shape once mixed
products.verdict: For quick hardware fixes that require real holding power, this tiny epoxy stick outperforms general-purpose fillers — just confirm freshness before relying on it.
products.review:
Where most wood fillers focus on patching surface defects, the KwikWood Stick is built for load-bearing hardware repairs. It delivers 900 PSI lap shear strength and cures in an hour, making it a better fit for anchoring screws than the top pick’s general-purpose smoothing putty. It sands and drills easily once hard, so you can reinstall hardware the same day.

This is for DIYers fixing stripped screw holes, loose hinges, or small structural cracks that need to hold fasteners. The 1 oz stick size and rapid 15-25 minute set time make it unsuitable for large surface fills or cosmetic work. Open the stick and check for a fresh, pliable interior — older stock can arrive dry and crumbly. The quick cure demands immediate shaping; you won’t have time to leisurely work it into a wide gap.

💡 💡 Tip: Have the repair area prepped and shape the putty immediately; you have roughly 15 minutes to work before it stiffens.

products.title: DAP Plastic Wood-X with DryDex Wood Filler, Natural, 5.5 Oz (7079800540)
products.label: DAP DryDex Wood Filler
products.badge: Best for Small Nail Holes with Visual Dry Indicator
products.oneLiner: Pink-to-natural color change signals dryness, eliminating guesswork on nail holes.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: Apply in thin layers no thicker than 1/8 inch; wait until each layer turns completely natural before adding more.
pros: Pink-to-natural color change shows when filler is dry and ready to sand. — Color change topic 23 mentions, 74% positive
Sands easily and accepts paint without swelling or roughness. — Sandability 35 mentions, 97% positive; Ease of use 37 mentions, 95% positive
Small fills resist shrinking, leaving a smooth flush surface. — Functionality 66 mentions, 95% positive
cons: The 5.5-oz tub is roughly as large as an espresso cup, limiting use to very minor touch-ups. — Container size noted as espresso-cup size in feedback.
Deep fills may need up to 36 hours to fully cure, and can still crack if not applied in thin layers. — Drying time feedback indicates extended cure for deep applications.
products.verdict: For a quick-dab touch-up kit around the house, the DAP DryDex delivers a confidence-boosting visual cue that no other filler in this lineup offers. Just don't expect it to handle deep gouges or supply a full workshop.
products.review:
The DryDex formula turns pink when applied, and lightens to a natural wood tone as it dries. This prevents sanding too soon, a typical hassle for new DIYers. Unlike a solvent-based pre-colored putty that relies on a specific stain shade, this latex filler is designed for painted interiors, where the color-change feature acts as a built-in progress indicator.

This filler is best reserved for very small jobs — nail holes, scratches, and tiny gouges — and is intended for paint-grade surfaces, not stained wood. The container is notably small, about the size of a single-serve espresso cup, so it won't last through a full project like baseboard installation. Deep fills are also a poor fit: they can take up to 36 hours to cure fully and may crack if rushed. For a kitchen or bathroom touch-up kit where you just need to dab a few holes, the convenience of the dry indicator outweighs the limited quantity.

💡 💡 Tip: Apply in thin layers no thicker than 1/8 inch; wait until each layer turns completely natural before adding more.

products.title: PC Products PC-Woody Wood Repair Epoxy Paste, Two-Part 12 oz in Two Cans, Tan
products.label: PC-Woody Epoxy Paste 12 oz
products.badge: Best Epoxy with Extended Working Time
products.oneLiner: 12-oz two-part epoxy gives extra shaping time for complex rot repairs.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: Keep a damp rag and cup of water nearby when mixing to manage the stickiness and prevent tools from fouling quickly.
pros: Strong structural bonding for rotted or missing wood sections — Effectiveness 305 mentions, 92% positive; Strength 144 mentions, 78% positive
Extended open time that allows careful shaping and carving before curing — Drying time 93 mentions, 75% positive
Sands, shapes, and paints well once cured, blending seamlessly into exterior woodwork — Sandability 72 mentions, 71% positive
cons: Components may be stiff and hard to combine evenly if stored in a cold environment or past its prime — Mixability 108 mentions, 29% negative
Extremely sticky and messy to mix and apply; can adhere to tools and gloves — 178 mentions, 59% negative
products.verdict: If you need extensive shaping time for rot repairs and can tolerate the sticky mess, this epoxy paste delivers a tough, long-lasting repair.
products.review:
PC-Woody gives you an extended working window for shaping rot repairs on exterior trim, thresholds, and door frames. Unlike the fast-setting alternative, you have time to carve and sand the epoxy before it hardens to a rock-solid, paintable finish. Once cured, it sands and shapes cleanly, holding paint well without shrinking or cracking under weather exposure. The epoxy remains water resistant, making it a durable choice for exterior use. It excels on rotted window sills and door bottoms.

It suits anyone tackling deep rot where careful sculpting matters, provided you can handle the trade-off: the paste is extremely tacky, clinging to tools and gloves like hot tar. Preparing a clean workspace and using disposable mixing sticks and gloves can keep frustrations manageable. For those who need a tough, structural repair that outlasts the surrounding wood, the extra cleanup effort is a worthwhile sacrifice. This is not for quick nail-hole fills or indoor repairs where odor and mess are dealbreakers.

💡 💡 Tip: Keep a damp rag and cup of water nearby when mixing to manage the stickiness and prevent tools from fouling quickly.

Where the top pick covers larger paint-grade jobs with a generous tub, this tube format prioritizes speed for quick trim repairs. The creamy filler spreads smoothly and can be shaped with a wet fingertip, minimizing sanding before paint. It hardens enough to sand in an hour, so you can complete the repair and repaint in the same afternoon. Water cleanup is a plus when you’re tidying up around baseboards.

This is the filler for touching up nail holes in painted baseboards, crown molding, and interior trim where a small amount goes a long way. The 3.25-ounce tube won’t carry a full room reno — it’s a spot-repair item, not a whole-room solution. Stay clear of structural gaps or anything load-bearing; this formulation is purely cosmetic.

💡 💡 Tip: Warm the tube in your hands for a few minutes before use to make squeezing easier.

Deeper gouges are beyond its design scope — it performs best on surface-level cosmetic fixes like nail holes, scratches, and narrow cracks.

The Gorilla Wood Filler sands to a smooth, paint-ready surface and costs little for a 16 oz tub, making it attractive for nail holes and trim repairs before repainting. However, the tub may arrive overly dry or hardened, leaving some material unusable. Durability after curing can also be questionable, with occasional crumbling. For cosmetic indoor fill work on painted surfaces where cost and sandability matter most, this can be a useful option if you can verify freshness at purchase.

💡 💡 Tip: Work in small dabs and smooth quickly — it sets up faster than typical water-based fillers.

buyingGuide.heading: Choose the Right Filler Type
buyingGuide.body:
Latex and PVA putties sand easily and clean up with water, making them ideal for paint-grade interior trim. Epoxy fillers (sticks or two-part pastes) cure into a hard, structural patch that can be drilled and will resist rot outdoors. Solvent-based fillers offer a traditional, hard finish but come with strong fumes and hand-cleanup hassle.

PVA or water-based putties are the most forgiving for shallow cosmetic work, while epoxies are the only choice when strength or outdoor exposure is non-negotiable.

buyingGuide.heading: Balance Drying Speed with Working Window
buyingGuide.body:
Fast-drying fillers reduce downtime but can grab your tool within a minute. If you need to shape a patch, look for 'extended open time' products that give you several minutes. For nail holes, a quick 15-minute sandable filler is fine; for sculpting a rotted sill, you'll regret a filler that hardens before you smooth it.

The hidden trade-off: many epoxies and Bondo set rapidly, demanding you mix only what you can apply in 60–90 seconds. In contrast, a latex like FamoWood remains workable long enough to feather edges.

buyingGuide.heading: Stainable vs. Paint-Grade Fillers
buyingGuide.body:
'Stainable' on the label rarely means the filler absorbs stain like the surrounding wood. True stain-grade fillers (like Timbermate) are engineered to accept pigment evenly. For anything else, plan to paint over the repair, because even slight color differences jump out under clear finishes.

If you are staining a project, test the filler on scrap wood first—even products marketed as stainable can leave a lighter or darker spot that destroys the look.

buyingGuide.heading: Indoor vs. Outdoor Use and Water Resistance
buyingGuide.body:
Labels that say 'water-resistant' or 'exterior' on a latex filler offer limited protection. For any repair that sees rain, snow, or ground contact, a two-part epoxy (Bondo, PC-Woody) is the only reliable barrier against moisture infiltration and freeze-thaw damage.

Using a standard interior filler outdoors usually leads to cracking or crumbling within a single season.

buyingGuide.heading: Tube, Tub, or Jar: Dispensing and Shelf Life
buyingGuide.body:
Tubes are convenient for small touch-ups but often dry out before you finish the product—especially budget tubes like Minwax or certain DAP formulas. Tubs give you more material at a better volume, but may require stirring and can harden if the lid isn't sealed tightly. Jars with airtight lids offer the longest shelf life for occasional DIYers.

If you only fill a few nail holes per year, a small jar or tube that stays fresh is more economical than a large tub that turns solid.

faq.question: Why does my wood filler still show after staining?
faq.answer: Most fillers labeled 'stainable' absorb stain differently than real wood, leaving a patch that's either lighter or darker. To hide a repair, use a true stain-grade filler like Timbermate and always test on an inconspicuous area. For painted projects, skip stainable claims entirely and choose a paint-grade filler that sands smooth.

faq.question: How can I keep wood filler from cracking on deep holes?
faq.answer: Deep fills crack when a thick layer of filler shrinks unevenly. Apply filler in thin lifts, letting each layer dry before adding the next. For holes deeper than ¼ inch, a two-part epoxy like PC-Woody or Bondo is less shrinkage-prone and provides structural support.

faq.question: What's the difference between epoxy wood filler and latex wood filler for outdoor repairs?
faq.answer: Epoxy fillers (Bondo, PC-Woody) cure into a hard, waterproof patch that resists rot and expansion. Latex fillers may say 'exterior' but often absorb moisture over time, leading to cracking or failure. For any repair exposed to rain or ground contact, epoxy is the safer choice.

faq.question: Why is my Gorilla wood filler crumbly right out of the tub?
faq.answer: Gorilla's water-based acrylic filler sometimes dries out in the tub before you open it, especially if the seal wasn't airtight. If you get a fresh tub, stir and test the consistency. Avoid rehydrating a crumbly filler with water—it weakens the bond. Return dried-out product or choose a filler packaged in a jar with a better seal, like FamoWood.

faq.question: How do I manage wood filler that sets too fast?
faq.answer: Work in small batches and spread the filler within 1–2 minutes. For epoxy sticks, knead only the amount you can apply in 60 seconds. If you need more time, choose a product with extended open time like PC-Woody, or keep a damp finger or tool to smooth latex fillers before they crust over.
